contracts.ai_analysis:
## Suspicion Score: 6/10 — Structurally concerning, warrants scrutiny

This is not one contract — it's 56 separate master services agreements bundled under a single board approval, and it has grown dramatically since its original approval. Reading the official description carefully tells the real story:

- **Original contract (Board Report 142-20/21):** $16,000,000
- **1st authorized increase (Board Report 071-21/22):** $152,000,000
- **2nd authorized increase (this approval, June 2025):** $36,000,000
- **Total aggregate value:** $204,000,000

What started as a $16M contract in 2020-2021 grew to $204M — a 1,175% increase over the contract's life. Each increase required only a board vote, not a new competitive bidding process. This is a legitimate procurement mechanism but it means 56 vendors were originally selected to share $16M, and then LAUSD progressively authorized them to do 12 times more work without re-competing the contracts.

The contract's listed dates — start January 1, 2021 and end December 31, 2025 — combined with the June 2025 board approval of this latest increase means LAUSD approved a $36M expansion just 6 months before the contract was set to expire. That timing is worth noting.

## What the vendors are doing

The board document describes what these 56 vendors are actually doing: modernizing the Parent Portal, Principals' Portal, iFieldTrip, and eLibrary applications; implementing school network system upgrades; supporting enterprise reporting and enterprise resource planning (Ariba, Whole Child, Focus Reporting); and network infrastructure projects.

## Vendor spotlight

**Trinus Corporation** is a legitimate IT consulting firm that has held government cooperative contracts with the City of Los Angeles and the Texas Department of Information Resources. It has a public presence and a track record.

**Varsun eTechnologies** is a smaller, privately-held Anaheim-based IT company founded in 2003 by Rama Sayyaparaju, with offices also in Hyderabad, India. Annual revenue is estimated under $1M. This is a very small company on a very large contract bench. Its headquarters has moved between multiple addresses in Anaheim. The company has 100-250 employees globally according to LinkedIn. While being small isn't disqualifying, a company with under $1M in annual revenue on a $204M bench contract raises legitimate capacity questions.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- The contract grew from $16M to $204M. What triggered the need for each increase? Were any of the scope expansions — particularly the "network system upgrades not originally contemplated" — subjected to any competitive review before being added?
- Which of the 56 vendors have actually received work orders, and how much has each been paid? The $204M is an aggregate ceiling — the actual spending per vendor is completely invisible without work order data. A PRA request for all work orders issued under contract C2613 would reveal this.
- Varsun eTechnologies has estimated annual revenue under $1M. How much has LAUSD paid Varsun under this contract since 2021? If a company with sub-$1M revenue is receiving millions in LAUSD payments, that's a significant concentration of revenue that warrants scrutiny.
- The description mentions Ariba (SAP's procurement platform) — SAP Public Services holds a separate $25.5M LAUSD contract (ID 58). Are any of these 56 IT staff augmentation vendors also providing services related to the SAP/Ariba implementation, creating overlap with the SAP contract?
- Who at LAUSD is managing 56 separate vendor relationships? Without a strong contract management function, a bench this large becomes impossible to oversee effectively.
contracts.red_flags:
- **1,175% cost growth:** The contract grew from $16M to $204M. While capacity increases are normal for staff augmentation benches, this scale of growth without re-competition is the single largest accountability gap in this contract. The original 56 vendors were selected to compete for $16M worth of work — they are now collectively authorized for $204M.
- **Small vendor capacity mismatch (Varsun eTechnologies):** A company with under $1M in estimated annual revenue appearing on a contract bench worth $204M total is unusual. This doesn't mean wrongdoing — SBE (Small Business Enterprise) participation is often required on large public contracts — but the work order data would tell you whether Varsun has been paid proportionally to its size or whether it's receiving large payments that dwarf its apparent capacity.
- **Timing of the $36M increase:** LAUSD approved a $36M capacity increase in June 2025 on a contract expiring December 2025. This is essentially a 6-month, $36M authorization issued six months before the contract ends. What specifically required this last-minute increase?
- **56 vendors, zero individual accountability:** With 56 vendors sharing one contract number and one aggregate ceiling, there is no public record of what any individual vendor has been paid. This structure is the hardest type of contract to scrutinize from outside the district.

contracts.ai_analysis: A $78.35M bench contract covering 15 vendors providing cybersecurity software. This is a massive commitment to a category that appears multiple times in the contract database. Cybersecurity spending appears in at least three separate LAUSD contract categories: this software bench ($78M), the cybersecurity staffing bench ($222M), and the IT Infrastructure master services bench ($250M). LAUSD may be paying for cybersecurity three times over with no consolidated accountability. In 2022, LAUSD suffered a major ransomware attack by Vice Society that exposed sensitive student and employee data including medical conditions and abuse allegations. Despite this breach and the subsequent massive increase in cybersecurity spending, there is no public report documenting what failures caused the breach or whether the new spending addresses them.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- What is the total amount LAUSD spends on cybersecurity across all contracts and who oversees the consolidated strategy?
- Following the 2022 ransomware attack, what independent security audit was conducted and was it published?
- Which of the 15 vendors on this bench have received purchase orders and for what specific products?
- Is there overlap between this software bench and cybersecurity items in the IT master services bench?
contracts.red_flags:
- Cybersecurity spending appears in at least three separate LAUSD contract categories with no consolidated accounting — total cybersecurity spend may exceed $350M.
- 15 vendors sharing $78.35M with no public breakdown of individual vendor spending or what specific protections each provides.
- In 2022, LAUSD suffered a ransomware attack exposing sensitive student data. No public post-mortem was published and no accountability report links the breach to the new spending.
- The 2022 breach exposed approximately 2,000 students' data including medical conditions and abuse allegations.
contracts.cheaper_alternative: California's statewide cybersecurity contracts through the Department of Technology offer negotiated rates for public agencies. The MS-ISAC (Multi-State Information Sharing and Analysis Center) provides free cybersecurity services to K-12 districts through DHS.
contracts.overlap_note: Direct overlap with cybersecurity line items in the $250M IT master services bench and the $222M cybersecurity staffing bench. Total cybersecurity-adjacent spending across these three contracts could exceed $550M.

## Ownership

ConvergeOne is a PE-backed IT services company owned by **CVC Capital Partners**. It provides network infrastructure, cybersecurity, and communications technology to large organizations.

## Controversies

**Bankruptcy:** ConvergeOne filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in April 2024 with approximately $1.8 billion in secured debt. [Sidley Austin LLP](https://www.sidley.com/) The company emerged from bankruptcy but the restructuring itself became a landmark legal case.

**Court ruling:** In September 2025, a federal court reversed part of ConvergeOne's bankruptcy plan, finding that the company's private equity sponsor CVC Capital Partners was given preferential treatment — an exclusive opportunity to purchase discounted equity that was not offered to all creditors equally, violating the US Bankruptcy Code. contracts.title: CDW Chromebook Content Filtering
contracts.description: Approval of a formally competed contract procured through an Invitation for Bid (IFB) process, to provide a Districtwide content filtering solution for approximately 365,000 District-issued Chromebooks. The GoGuardian content filtering solution, provided by CDW Government LLC, is essential for ensuring compliance with the Children’s Internet Protection Act (CIPA) and supports District-wide efforts to promote safe, responsible, and effective technology use in the classroom. Three bids were received for this solicitation, and one was deemed qualified. The evaluation committee was comprised of staff from Information Technology Services. CDW Government LLC (CDW-G) was the lowest, responsive, and responsible bidder. CDW-G has been doing business with the District since 2013. Examples of other clients are Clark County School District and the New York City Department of Education. CDW-G has extensive experience partnering with K–12 school districts to enhance technological infrastructure and improve educational outcomes. This action aligns with the District’s Strategic Plan Pillar 4: Operational Effectiveness.
contracts.notes: Board Report: 325-24/25. Extracted from Legistar API on 2026-03-15
contracts.ai_analysis: CDW Government is providing Chromebook content filtering software — tools that prevent students from accessing inappropriate content on school-issued Chromebooks. CDW is appearing on yet another LAUSD contract. Combined with the hotspot contract ($72M) and its presence on the IT master services bench ($250M), CDW's total LAUSD exposure is significant and not publicly consolidated. Content filtering for Google Chromebooks is a commoditized function. Google's own admin console includes content filtering tools at no additional cost as part of Google Workspace for Education — a license LAUSD almost certainly already pays for. Paying CDW $1.7M for content filtering that Google provides for free inside an existing license is a significant accountability question.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- Does LAUSD have an active Google Workspace for Education license? If so, does it include the content filtering features this contract provides?
- What specific capabilities does this contract provide that aren't available through Google's built-in Chromebook admin tools?
- What is CDW's markup on the underlying filtering software versus direct purchase from the manufacturer?
contracts.red_flags:
- Google Workspace for Education includes built-in content filtering and admin controls for Chromebooks at no additional cost. If LAUSD already has this license, this $1.7M contract may be duplicative.
- CDW appears on at least three LAUSD contracts simultaneously with no consolidated accounting.
- Content filtering is a commoditized function — paying a reseller markup for it is difficult to justify.
contracts.cheaper_alternative: Google Workspace for Education includes SafeSearch enforcement, content filtering, and admin controls for Chromebooks at no additional cost as part of the base license. If LAUSD already pays for Google Workspace — which it almost certainly does — this contract may be paying for something already included.
contracts.overlap_note: CDW also holds the $72M hotspot contract and appears on the IT master services bench. This content filtering contract likely overlaps with Google Workspace for Education licenses LAUSD already pays for.

ConvergeOne is PE-backed and was paid more than its original contracted amount — consistent with the broader pattern the PESP report identified where nearly 70% of PE-backed LAUSD vendors were paid over contract. Network modernization infrastructure contracts are difficult to audit because the work is technical and distributed across hundreds of school sites.

contracts.ai_analysis: Cal/AMP dba Synovia provides GPS fleet tracking for LAUSD's school bus fleet integrated with the LAUSD app's Here Comes the Bus feature. The system doesn't work well. Multiple parents and a 33-year LAUSD bus driver reported the GPS was only 50% accurate. One parent reported her daughter got on the wrong bus — the Synovia app showed the bus in Hollywood when it was actually elsewhere. Parents are using personal Apple AirTags as a more reliable backup. LAUSD's spokesperson simultaneously insisted the system was 'operating at full capacity with no disruptions.' This disconnect between official claims and documented parent experience is a governance problem. LAUSD has 2,700 bus routes serving 43,000 students daily.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- What accuracy standard does the Synovia contract require and what are the financial penalties for non-performance?
- Has LAUSD conducted a formal accuracy audit independent of the vendor's own reporting?
- What performance benchmarks were established at contract signing and are they being met?
- Has LAUSD evaluated rebidding this contract to competitors with transparent per-bus pricing?
contracts.red_flags:
- Multiple parents and a veteran bus driver report the GPS is only 50% accurate. Parents are using personal AirTags as a backup because they don't trust the district system.
- LAUSD's spokesperson claimed the system was 'operating at full capacity with no disruptions' while parents simultaneously reported it was broken — a documented disconnect between official claims and reality.
- A competitor called SpotMyBus charges $34 per bus per month with transparent pricing — Synovia avoids publishing its rates.
- No performance standards or accuracy requirements appear in the public contract record.
contracts.cheaper_alternative: SpotMyBus charges $34 per bus per month with transparent pricing versus Synovia's opaque enterprise rates. At 2,700 routes, SpotMyBus would cost approximately $1.1M annually — higher than this contract but with published pricing and comparable features. The real question is whether this contract includes performance standards with financial penalties for failure.
contracts.overlap_note: No direct overlap with other contracts but the GPS data feeds into the LAUSD app's parent portal — which has separate technology contracts. The integration should be documented.

contracts.ai_analysis:
This contract is for Heartland School Solutions' payment platform (MySchoolBucks), covering electronic payments district-wide: credit cards, debit cards, Google Pay, Apple Pay, and ACH across all LAUSD schools and offices. The $1M contract value is for LAUSD's implementation and software access — but that is not the real money, and it may not even be necessary money.

California made school meals free for every student in 2022 — breakfast and lunch, no application required. LAUSD has since gone further, covering AP exam fees for all students as of 2025-26. The two biggest categories of payments that historically justified systems like MySchoolBucks no longer exist at LAUSD.

What remains are field trips, sports registration, club dues, parking passes, and after-school program fees — small, low-volume transactions that California Education Code already prohibits making mandatory for educational activities. LAUSD is paying $1M for a payment processing system to collect fees that either don't exist anymore, legally can't be mandatory, or are small enough that the cost of collecting them might rival the revenue they generate.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- What is the total dollar volume of transactions processed through MySchoolBucks at LAUSD annually? This is the question the entire contract justification rests on, and it cannot be answered from the board document. A PRA request for all MySchoolBucks transaction volume data since 2021 is needed. If the answer is $2–3M in field trip and activity fees across 448,000 students, LAUSD is spending $1M in direct contract fees — plus an unknown additional sum extracted from families in transaction fees — to move a relatively modest amount of money.
- The district already decided AP exam fees were worth absorbing directly. If the remaining payment volume is small enough, the cheaper solution may be to simply eliminate the fees the same way, rather than maintain an elaborate payment infrastructure that costs families more than it costs the district.
- The contract says fees are passed to parents "similar to how a car registration payment works with the California DMV." Did the LAUSD board understand that approving this contract was implicitly authorizing a system that charges LAUSD families — particularly low-income families — at rates the federal government has called disproportionate and potentially illegal?
- The $18.25M settlement was finalized in September 2025. LAUSD's board approved this contract in April 2025. Was the pending class action disclosed to the board? Does the contract include any fee cap protections?
- Heartland raised its fees unilaterally in March 2024 with district notification but no ability for districts to negotiate. Does LAUSD's contract include fee caps, or can Heartland raise transaction fees on LAUSD families at will?
- The contract transferred to Acrisure in October 2025. Did LAUSD receive a formal assignment notice, and did the district's legal office review whether consent was required?
- Heartland also holds the Cafeteria Software contract (#28, $600K). Between the two contracts, LAUSD is paying Heartland $1.6M directly while Heartland simultaneously extracts an unknown but likely larger sum from LAUSD families. What is the total amount LAUSD families have paid in MySchoolBucks transaction fees since the prior contract began in 2021?
contracts.red_flags:
- **Private equity backed.**
- **Fee model designed to extract from low-income families:** The CFPB explicitly found that MySchoolBucks' flat-fee structure disproportionately burdens low-income families who make small, frequent deposits. LAUSD's student population skews heavily toward low-income families. This is not a neutral fee structure for this community.
- **Fees raised unilaterally mid-contract:** In 2024, Heartland raised its per-transaction fee to $3.25 with no district negotiation. LAUSD families absorbed the increase with no recourse.
- **Ownership changed six months post-signing:** The entity LAUSD contracted with (Global Payments) no longer owns this product. It now sits inside Acrisure, a PE-backed conglomerate that was not vetted during procurement.
- **Active litigation at time of signing:** The class action settlement was finalized in September 2025. LAUSD signed in April 2025 while the case was pending. Whether this was disclosed to the board is unknown.
- **Combined Heartland exposure is $1.6M direct + uncapped family fees:** The board line item understates the true financial relationship with this vendor by design.

Bench contracts allow schools to select from an approved vendor list without going through individual procurement. 33 vendors sharing a $10M ceiling makes individual vendor accountability nearly impossible. contracts.ai_analysis:
## Why this contract exists — the 2022 ransomware attack

This contract is the direct legacy of one of the largest school cyberattacks in US history. On September 3, 2022, LAUSD fell victim to a ransomware attack by a Russian-speaking cybercriminal gang known as **Vice Society**, which encrypted files and stole data from the second-largest school district in the country, triggering an unprecedented shutdown of computer systems and prompting urgent calls with the White House and the National Security Council. [NPR](https://www.npr.org/2022/09/06/1121340584/los-angeles-unified-school-district-ransomware-cyberattack)

Two years before the attack, auditors had already warned LAUSD about significant cybersecurity weaknesses. In 2020, cybersecurity auditors successfully tricked LAUSD employees into sharing their passwords and "unknowingly executing malicious codes," and the confidential report sent to CIO Soheil Katal contained 38 findings including "significant risks around passwords and credentials." [LAist](https://laist.com/news/education/lausd-cybersecurity-audit-warnings-before-hack)

LAUSD's systems face 33.5 million attempted malware attacks annually. The district has 250 applications, 1.6 million active directory users, and approximately 130,000 networked devices. [Government Technology](https://www.govtech.com/education/k-12/lausd-cybersecurity) The scale of this contract reflects the scale of the threat.

## Is this spending justified?

Partially. Cybersecurity is a genuine and documented need for LAUSD — this is not discretionary spending like iReady. However several specific vendors in this 35-company bench warrant scrutiny:

## Infosys — a vendor being paid to protect LAUSD's data that had its own massive breach

Infosys Limited is one of the 35 vendors on this contract. A class-action lawsuit filed in September 2024 named both LAUSD and Infosys, alleging that Infosys managed the My Integrated Student Information System — LAUSD's primary student data portal — which was compromised in the 2022 attack. The complaint alleges insufficient cybersecurity protocols allowed the intrusion to go unnoticed for more than two months. [The 74 Million](https://www.the74million.org/article/three-cyberattacks-exposed-data-on-millions-of-students-now-lausd-faces-a-class-action-lawsuit/)

Separately, Infosys's US subsidiary **Infosys McCamish Systems** agreed to pay $17.5 million to settle six class action lawsuits related to a 2023 data breach that compromised the personal information of more than 6 million people [BankInfoSecurity](https://www.bankinfosecurity.com/infosys-mccamish-settles-breach-lawsuits-for-175-million-a-27201) — including Bank of America and Fidelity customers. The ransomware group LockBit was responsible. In other words: LAUSD is paying a company for cybersecurity services that was itself successfully ransomwared and that is named in litigation related to LAUSD's own breach.

## MGT of America — already embedded through emergency contracts

MGT Technology Solutions was brought in to help LAUSD recover from the 2022 cyberattack [MGT](https://www.mgtconsulting.com) and is now one of the 35 vendors on this ongoing bench. The connection between the emergency response contract and this long-term bench contract is worth examining — was MGT competitively selected for the bench, or did their emergency role give them an inside track?

## The structure of the contract — 35 vendors, $222M — creates accountability gaps

With 35 vendors sharing a single contract ceiling, it's extremely difficult to track which vendor is doing what work, at what cost, and with what results. The board approval authorizes up to $101.2 million in increases or decreases across the aggregate — meaning individual vendor spending is largely invisible to public scrutiny. This structure is legitimate for staff augmentation but creates minimal accountability for outcomes.

## The three cyberattacks context

LAUSD has experienced three significant cyber incidents: the September 2022 Vice Society ransomware attack that exposed students' psychological evaluations; a January 2022 attack on ed tech vendor Illuminate Education; and a massive June 2024 cyberattack on cloud computing company Snowflake, a third-party vendor used by the district to store certain records. [The 74 Million](https://www.the74million.org/article/three-cyberattacks-exposed-data-on-millions-of-students-now-lausd-faces-a-class-action-lawsuit/) Despite the 2022 attack and the subsequent cybersecurity investment, the district was breached again in 2024.

**Sources:** [The 74 — Three LAUSD Cyberattacks](https://www.the74million.org/article/three-cyberattacks-exposed-data-on-millions-of-students-now-lausd-faces-a-class-action-lawsuit/) · [NPR — 2022 Ransomware Attack](https://www.npr.org/2022/09/06/1121340584/los-angeles-unified-school-district-ransomware-cyberattack) · [LAist — Pre-Attack Audit](https://laist.com/news/education/lausd-cybersecurity-audit-warnings-before-hack) · [Infosys Settlement](https://www.bankinfosecurity.com/infosys-mccamish-settles-breach-lawsuits-for-175-million-a-27201) · [MGT/LAUSD Recovery](https://www.mgtconsulting.com)
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- Is $222.5M over five years the right amount to spend on cybersecurity staffing, and is a 35-vendor bench the right structure?
- How was Infosys selected for this contract given it is named in litigation related to LAUSD's own 2022 breach?
- Was MGT competitively selected for the bench, or did their emergency response role give them an inside track?
- What has each of the 35 vendors actually been paid under this contract?
contracts.red_flags:
- **Infosys conflict of interest:** LAUSD is paying Infosys for cybersecurity services while Infosys is named in a class-action lawsuit alleging its negligence contributed to LAUSD's 2022 data breach. Infosys's own subsidiary paid $17.5M to settle a separate breach affecting 6 million people.
- **MGT embedded vendor:** MGT was brought in for emergency recovery after the 2022 attack and is now on the long-term bench — potential inside track.
- **35-vendor bench with $222M ceiling:** Individual vendor spending is invisible without specific work order data. Board authorized $101.2M in increases/decreases across the aggregate.
- **Three breaches despite investment:** LAUSD was breached again in 2024 via Snowflake despite the post-2022 cybersecurity investment.

contracts.keywords: CDW, hotspot, devices, private equity, Madison Dearborn Partners, PE-backed, reseller
contracts.ai_analysis: CDW Government is purchasing and supplying internet hotspot devices to LAUSD students. CDW is a $20+ billion revenue technology reseller — it buys devices from manufacturers and resells them to government clients at a markup. CDW appears on multiple LAUSD contracts simultaneously: this hotspot contract ($72M), the IT master services bench ($250M shared with 11 other vendors), and separately on the cybersecurity software bench. Total CDW exposure across LAUSD contracts is not publicly documented. CDW Government is a subsidiary of CDW Corporation, which has $4 billion in debt from a 2007 private equity leveraged buyout. Reseller markups partially service that debt.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- What markup is CDW charging over manufacturer cost and has LAUSD compared that to direct manufacturer pricing?
- What is CDW's total contract exposure across all LAUSD contracts combined?
- What percentage of purchased hotspot devices are actively in use?
- Has LAUSD maximized E-rate reimbursement for these devices?
contracts.red_flags:
- CDW appears on at least three separate LAUSD contracts simultaneously with no consolidated accounting of total vendor exposure.
- CDW is a reseller, not a manufacturer. Its markup over direct manufacturer pricing is not disclosed. Similar reseller arrangements have cost districts 15-25% more than direct procurement.
- CDW Corporation carries $4 billion in PE-era debt. District payments flow through to service that debt.
- No published utilization report showing what percentage of purchased hotspot devices are actively in use.
contracts.cheaper_alternative: T-Mobile, Verizon, and AT&T all have direct K-12 hotspot programs with negotiated rates. California's statewide telecommunications contracts through the Department of Technology may offer lower rates than CDW's reseller markup. E-rate federal subsidies could also significantly reduce net cost.
contracts.overlap_note: CDW also appears on the IT master services bench and cybersecurity bench. Total CDW contract exposure across all LAUSD contracts is not publicly consolidated.
contracts.hook: $72M to a technology reseller that appears on at least three separate LAUSD contracts simultaneously. CDW adds a markup to manufacturer prices. LAUSD has never published a utilization report showing how many devices are actually in use.

contracts.title: Fleet Towing Services
contracts.description: Approval of a formally competed contract procured through an Invitation for Bid (IFB) process, to provide medium and heavy-duty towing services for LAUSD’s yellow and white fleets (medium and heavy duty size). This contract will ensure that inoperable buses and trucks, including electric models, are efficiently and safely towed back to designated bus yards, where they can be properly assessed and undergo necessary repairs to restore them to full operational status. These towing services will play a critical role in minimizing downtime and ensuring that the LAUSD fleet remains fully functional and available for daily operations. Without approval, inoperable vehicles would be left on city streets, potentially causing significant congestion and traffic delays for other motorists. This could lead to a bottleneck effect, slowing down the flow of traffic and increasing frustration for drivers. Additionally, the Transportation Services Branch would face limited resources to efficiently remove these vehicles and transport them to appropriate locations. contracts.title: Sales Tax Recovery Services
contracts.description: Approval of a formally competed contract, procured through a Request for Proposals (RFP) process, to provide sales tax recovery services. The services involve reviewing purchases and financial records to identify instances where the District may have overpaid or incorrectly paid sales taxes. These services ensure the District is only paying what is legally required and assists in recovering any eligible refunds. Sales tax laws can be complex with varying exemptions for school districts. This specialized service ensures compliance while taking advantage of all applicable exemptions. Recovering overpaid sales tax means more funds can be directed to educational programs, classroom resources, transportation, or other critical areas. Instances where the District may have incorrectly paid taxes include interstate purchases where buying goods or services across state lines may involve different tax rules, creating confusion about what is taxable; mixed-use services like construction and IT support that may involve taxable and non-taxable components where the labor for building maintenance may be exempt but the materials used could be taxable; when services are bundled with goods, making it difficult to determine how to separate the taxable components; and fuel tax exemptions which may apply to vehicles or equipment used directly for education purposes, like school buses. The fees for the contract are on a contingency basis and will be paid with the funds recovered as a result of the professional work rendered.

contracts.ai_analysis: iDREAM Mobile Tech Labs are expandable rolling units — computer labs on wheels — that can be deployed to schools that lack fixed computer infrastructure. Mobile tech labs became a common pandemic-era procurement. Many districts that bought them found they were underutilized once students returned to classrooms. The UNESCO 2023 report found that 67% of educational software licenses go unused nationally — mobile hardware units face similar utilization questions. contracts.title: IT Infrastructure Services
contracts.notes: Board Report: Rep-107-25/26. Extracted from Legistar API on 2026-03-15
contracts.ai_analysis:
Note: The $250M figure previously shown for this contract has been corrected following reader review. That amount belongs to a separate outside legal counsel services bench (Item G on the same board agenda). We are researching the correct contract amount for IT infrastructure services and will update when verified.

## What this contract is

A 12-vendor bench approved November 18, 2025, providing IT infrastructure master services to LAUSD. Unlike the cybersecurity contract which has a clear origin story (the 2022 ransomware attack), this contract's scope — "IT infrastructure master services" — is broad enough to cover almost anything technology-related, making it the hardest contract in the database to evaluate for necessity or value.

## Private equity runs through this list

At least three of the twelve vendors are PE-owned, meaning a portion of every dollar flows to investor returns:

**Presidio** was acquired by Clayton Dubilier & Rice (CD&R) in April 2024, with BC Partners retaining a minority stake. This was Presidio's fourth private equity owner — it has passed through American Securities, Apollo Global Management, and BC Partners since being founded in 2003. [Presidio](https://www.presidio.com) BC Partners was exploring a sale of Presidio that could value the company at more than $4 billion including debt. [Yahoo!](https://finance.yahoo.com) Every LAUSD dollar spent with Presidio services debt from a $4 billion leveraged buyout.

**CDW Government** is a subsidiary of CDW Corporation (NASDAQ: CDW), a publicly traded $20B+ revenue technology products reseller. It appears in this contract AND separately in contract ID 43 ($72M for hotspot devices) — meaning CDW has at least two separate LAUSD contracts running simultaneously worth a combined $72M+.

## Digital Scepter — the smallest and least-known vendor

Digital Scepter is a small Murrieta, California company with 98 LinkedIn followers and no significant public profile. It also appears separately in your database as contract ID 70 ($3.36M). A small local IT security firm appearing on both a $250M master services bench and holding its own separate $3.36M contract warrants a closer look at its ownership and any connections to LAUSD decision-makers. This is exactly the kind of vendor that should be researched via PRA — specifically, who at LAUSD recommended or championed this vendor.

## vCloud Tech — flagged in federal procurement databases

vCloud Tech is a small California-based IT reseller that has appeared on numerous government cooperative purchasing contracts. Small IT resellers on large government contracts are a known vector for markup inflation — the reseller adds a percentage on top of manufacturer prices with minimal value-add. Worth requesting the actual work orders under this contract to understand what vCloud Tech is specifically being paid to do.

## The "master services" structure problem

This is the same accountability concern as the cybersecurity bench. With 12 vendors sharing a $250M ceiling, individual vendor spending is invisible without specific work order data. The board approved $250M but the actual allocation to each vendor is determined by LAUSD staff — specifically CIO Soheil Katal's department — without further board approval. This is standard practice for large IT organizations but means the public has no visibility into whether, say, Digital Scepter received $500K or $50M of that $250M.

## The overlap problem

Several vendors appear on both this contract AND the cybersecurity bench (contract ID 41): CDW Government and Mainline Information Systems appear on both. When the same vendors are on multiple contracts simultaneously, it raises a legitimate question: are LAUSD's IT infrastructure needs genuinely served by consolidating to fewer, more accountable vendors, or does the bench structure benefit vendors by spreading spending across overlapping contracts?

**Sources:** [Presidio/CD&R acquisition](https://www.presidio.com) · Reuters PE ownership history · Presidio public sector page · Digital Scepter: [digitalscepter.com](https://digitalscepter.com)
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- What has CDW Government been paid across both contracts combined?
- Who at LAUSD has a prior relationship with Digital Scepter?
- What specific services has each of the 12 vendors actually delivered under this contract since November 2025?
- Why does a $250M contract have no listed start or end dates in your database — request the actual contract term via PRA
contracts.red_flags:
- Multiple PE-owned vendors (Presidio on its 4th PE owner, servicing debt from a $4B leveraged buyout with LAUSD dollars)
- Digital Scepter: tiny company (98 LinkedIn followers) appearing on both a $250M bench and a separate $3.36M contract — warrants PRA into who championed this vendor
- vCloud Tech: small IT reseller on a large government contract — a known vector for markup inflation
- 12 vendors sharing a $250M ceiling with no public visibility into individual vendor spending
- Vendor overlap with the cybersecurity bench (contract ID 41) — CDW Government and Mainline appear on both

contracts.notes: Board Report: Rep-107-25/26. Extracted from Legistar API on 2026-03-15
contracts.ai_analysis: SAP is LAUSD's enterprise resource planning system — the software that manages finance, HR, payroll, procurement, and accounting across the district. LAUSD has used SAP since at least 2011 and is currently migrating to SAP Ariba for procurement management. SAP is the world's most expensive ERP platform. Long-term legacy vendor relationships in ERP create switching costs that vendors exploit to prevent competitive bidding. The migration to SAP Ariba extends the district's dependency on a single vendor ecosystem, making future competitive bidding harder.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- When was SAP last put out for competitive bid and was an independent cost comparison conducted?
- What is the total cost of LAUSD's SAP ecosystem including implementation partners and customization?
- Has LAUSD evaluated whether California's statewide SAP contract rates are lower than what the district pays independently?
- What would it cost to migrate to a competing platform and has that analysis been done?
contracts.red_flags:
- SAP is the most expensive ERP platform on the market. There is no public record of LAUSD evaluating alternatives when renewing.
- LAUSD has been on SAP for over a decade — a dynamic SAP's pricing model is designed to create and exploit.
- The migration to SAP Ariba extends single-vendor dependency and makes future competitive bidding harder.
- Total cost of the SAP ecosystem including implementation partners, consultants, and customization is not publicly disclosed.
contracts.cheaper_alternative: California's statewide procurement system and existing state contracts with SAP may offer better pricing. Several large California school districts have successfully migrated to Workday at lower total cost.
contracts.overlap_note: SAP Ariba now manages vendor onboarding and purchase orders — functions that may overlap with the district's separate procurement staffing and contract administration workforce.

contracts.ai_analysis:
## Ownership

PowerSchool is a publicly traded company (NYSE: PWSC) that provides student information systems, learning management, and other software to K-12 schools. It has grown aggressively through acquisitions including Schoology (LMS), Naviance (college planning), and others.

## Controversies

**Major Data Breach:** In December 2024, PowerSchool suffered the largest breach of children's data in US history — more than 62 million student records and nearly 10 million teacher records were exfiltrated. [Tech Policy Press](https://techpolicy.press/) The compromised data included names, addresses, birthdates, Social Security numbers, medical conditions, disability accommodations, individualized education plans, disciplinary records, and family income data. [TechTarget](https://www.techtarget.com/)

PowerSchool confirmed it paid a ransom to the attackers. North Carolina's education department later stated that PowerSchool had assured customers the stolen data was destroyed — "Unfortunately, that has proven to be incorrect." [K-12 Dive](https://www.k12dive.com/)

**Ongoing extortion:** Threat actors subsequently contacted multiple school districts attempting to extort them using the same stolen data, even after PowerSchool paid the ransom. [PowerSchool](https://www.powerschool.com/)

**Legal exposure:** Over 100 districts have sued PowerSchool following the breach. [K-12 Dive](https://www.k12dive.com/) A 19-year-old student, Matthew Lane, pleaded guilty and was sentenced to four years in federal prison.

**Sources:** [TechPolicy.Press](https://techpolicy.press/) · [K-12 Dive](https://www.k12dive.com/) · [PowerSchool breach notice](https://www.powerschool.com/)
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- Was LAUSD student data compromised in the December 2024 PowerSchool breach?
- Does LAUSD use PowerSchool's Student Information System (the breached product) or only other PowerSchool products?
- Has LAUSD joined or considered joining the 100+ districts suing PowerSchool?
- What notification was provided to LAUSD parents about potential data exposure?
contracts.red_flags:
- **Largest children's data breach in US history:** 62 million student records and 10 million teacher records exfiltrated in December 2024, including SSNs, medical conditions, and disciplinary records.
- **Ransom paid, data not destroyed:** PowerSchool paid the ransom and assured customers data was destroyed — this later proved false. Extortion attempts continued against individual districts.
- **100+ districts suing:** Over 100 school districts have filed lawsuits against PowerSchool.
- **Critical question unanswered:** Whether LAUSD student data was specifically compromised has not been publicly confirmed.

This analysis is based on publicly available information including news reports, public records, and corporate disclosures. It is not a statement of wrongdoing. Verify all claims independently before acting on them.

## Ownership
Curriculum Associates is owned by Berkshire Partners, a Boston-based private equity firm that closed its most recent fund at $7.8 billion in 2024. The company has approximately 2,700 employees and an estimated $750 million in annual revenue — derived overwhelmingly from public school contracts.

## Effectiveness Research
**The product:** i-Ready is an adaptive diagnostic and instructional platform for reading and math. LAUSD replaced Renaissance STAR (used since 1998) with i-Ready in September 2023, covering approximately 448,174 students TK-12.

**LAUSD’s own admission:** A November 2023 LAUSD memo states explicitly that i-Ready personalized instruction is ‘not mandated’ and ‘not a substitute for teacher-led instruction.’ LAUSD is paying $20.2M for a platform it officially describes as optional.

**Free alternative exists:** California already provides diagnostic assessments through the CAASPP/SBAC system at no cost to districts.

**Questions repeat:** Students have reported memorizing the test questions because they do not change year to year — directly undermining the platform’s core value as a diagnostic tool. A 7th grader at Portola Middle School confirmed he has memorized the passage sequence: vocabulary words (incandescent, jibe, quip), then The Invisible Man, then presidential speeches, then a story about a woman who circumnavigated the earth on a bike — in the same order every time.

**Research concerns:** An independent analysis of three controlled studies found a mean effect size of .04 to .10 — below the .20 threshold considered educationally meaningful. Most research cited by Curriculum Associates is funded by Curriculum Associates itself.

## Controversies
**Federal lawsuit:** In 2025, Curriculum Associates was hit with a federal class-action lawsuit alleging the platform is primarily a data-extraction operation exploiting minors without adequate parental consent.

**Teacher opposition:** UTLA actively provides sample letters helping teachers push back on i-Ready use.

## Sources
Pitchbook · Teaching By Science · UTLA · Berkshire Partners · LAUSD Board Report 220-22/23
contracts.red_flags:
- **PE ownership:** Berkshire Partners ($7.8B fund) owns Curriculum Associates — $750M annual revenue derived primarily from public school contracts raises questions about whether product decisions prioritize student outcomes or investor returns.
- **Embedded vendor influence:** Curriculum Associates assigns a dedicated Partner Success Manager to work directly with district administration. This person’s professional success depends on adoption rates and contract renewal, not student outcomes.
- **LAUSD’s own memo contradicts the purchase:** The district officially says i-Ready is ‘not mandated’ and ‘not a substitute for teacher-led instruction’ — yet pays $20.2M for it.
- **Free alternative exists:** California’s CAASPP/SBAC provides diagnostic assessments at no cost.
- **Questionable effectiveness:** Independent research found effect sizes of .04 to .10, below the .20 threshold for educational significance. Most supporting research is industry-funded.
- **Federal lawsuit:** Class-action alleging data extraction from minors without adequate parental consent.
- **Static test content:** Students report memorizing questions that don’t change year to year.
- **Replaced without teacher input:** This contract replaced Renaissance STAR. Teachers were not consulted.

contracts.keywords: ContinuumCloud, special education, IEP, student records, Battery Ventures, behavioral health
contracts.ai_analysis: ContinuumCloud is the parent company of Welligent, LAUSD's electronic health records platform for behavioral health and special education services. ContinuumCloud was formed by Battery Ventures, a PE firm, to acquire and consolidate behavioral health software companies. Battery Ventures also invested in Paragon — a spyware company founded by former Israeli intelligence officers that supplies ICE with surveillance tools. In March 2026, the LAUSD board voted to cut ties with vendors connected to immigration enforcement. The district then admitted it was unaware of this connection. It is unclear whether the review required by that resolution has been completed for this contract. Student behavioral health and special education records are among the most sensitive data a school district holds — and this data is managed by a company owned by a PE firm with investments in surveillance technology.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- Has LAUSD completed the vendor review required by the March 2026 immigration enforcement resolution for this contract?
- What specific student data does ContinuumCloud hold and who at Battery Ventures or its portfolio companies has access to it?
- Is there overlap between ContinuumCloud's Welligent platform and Kokomo24/7's health tools?
- Given the board's ICE resolution, what is the current status of this contract?
contracts.red_flags:
- ContinuumCloud is owned by Battery Ventures, which also invested in Paragon spyware used by ICE to surveil immigrants — a connection LAUSD admitted it was unaware of.
- The board's March 2026 resolution requires review of vendors connected to immigration enforcement. It is unclear whether this contract has been reviewed.
- Student behavioral health and special education records — among the most sensitive student data — are held by a PE-owned company with surveillance technology connections.
- Welligent has $8M in annual revenue and 19 employees. LAUSD is paying $19.8M for a contract with a company that generates less annually than the contract itself.
contracts.cheaper_alternative: Several open-source and nonprofit electronic health record platforms serve school districts at significantly lower cost. California's statewide student health data systems may offer an alternative worth evaluating.
contracts.overlap_note: Kokomo24/7 also manages health and safety data for LAUSD including immunization tracking. There may be overlap between what ContinuumCloud/Welligent does and what Kokomo does. The district should document which system holds which data.
contracts.hook: $19.8M to a company owned by the same investment firm that backed Paragon — spyware used by ICE to surveil immigrants. The board voted to cut ties with ICE-connected vendors. This contract is still active.

contracts.title: Kokomo24/7
contracts.description: Telehealth platform for physical health issues (minor injuries, allergies, headaches) for LAUSD students; health data management system. Funded via federal pandemic relief.
contracts.notes: Data systems transition completed Jul-31-2023. Inspire Health Group handles mental health; Kokomo handles physical. $800K program.
contracts.source_url: Kokomo24/7 blog; AWS case study; LAUSD Page/19344
contracts.ai_analysis: Kokomo24/7 is a school health and safety platform LAUSD has used since 2018. Services include anonymous incident reporting (LASAR tipline), emergency alerts for staff, health and wellness tracking, immunization management, visitor management, and telehealth. An AWS case study documenting LAUSD's Kokomo implementation states it cut health data infrastructure costs by 50% compared to the previous system. At $800K, this is one of the more defensible ed-tech contracts — it provides documented cost savings, genuine safety value, and has a multi-year track record with the district. The main questions involve overlap with ContinuumCloud's Welligent platform and whether AWS infrastructure costs are embedded in Kokomo's pricing.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- What specific health data does Kokomo hold versus what ContinuumCloud/Welligent holds and is there overlap?
- Is AWS infrastructure cost embedded in Kokomo's pricing creating a double-payment situation?
- What clinical outcomes has the Kokomo telehealth pilot produced?
- Has LAUSD evaluated consolidating Kokomo and ContinuumCloud into a single health data platform?
contracts.red_flags:
- Kokomo uses AWS infrastructure — raising the question of whether LAUSD pays for AWS twice: once directly and again inside Kokomo's contract.
- ContinuumCloud/Welligent also manages student health data for LAUSD. Overlap between the two systems should be documented.
- Kokomo's expanding telehealth services raise the question of whether a safety platform company should be delivering clinical care.
contracts.cheaper_alternative: Not applicable — Kokomo already replaced a more expensive system and cut costs by 50%. The question is consolidation with other health contracts, not replacement.
contracts.overlap_note: ContinuumCloud/Welligent manages behavioral health and special education records. LACOE has a mental health infrastructure contract. All three touch student health data — the district should document which system holds which data and whether consolidation is possible.

contracts.title: Ameresco, Inc.
contracts.description: Design, install, and operate 2.7 MW of solar carport/shade structure PV systems and EV charging infrastructure at 5 LAUSD middle schools (Northridge, Pacoima, Sherman Oaks CES, Sun Valley Magnet, Sutter). 25-year performance guarantee.
contracts.notes: Board approved Feb-2025 per CA Govt Code §4217.10. Part of 21-school solar program. Net cost avoidance est. $45.7M; utility savings est. $168M over 25 years.
contracts.source_url: Business Wire May-2025; LAUSD Board agenda 02-10-25
contracts.ai_analysis: Ameresco (NYSE: AMRC) is installing 2.7 megawatts of solar carport and EV charging infrastructure at five LAUSD middle schools as part of a larger 21-school program. The contract includes a 25-year operations and maintenance agreement. This is one of the more defensible contracts in the database — solar infrastructure generates measurable long-term utility cost savings. The 21-school program projects up to $168M in utility cost avoidance over 25 years against $45.7M in total program costs. However, that projection is Ameresco's own estimate, not independently audited, and the 25-year guarantee locks LAUSD into a single-vendor relationship for a generation.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- Were Ameresco's energy savings projections independently verified before the contract was signed?
- Was this project competitively bid and did LADWP's solar programs get evaluated?
- What happens to LAUSD's obligations if Ameresco is acquired before the 25-year guarantee ends?
contracts.red_flags:
- The $168M savings projection is Ameresco's own estimate — not independently audited or verified.
- A 25-year performance guarantee locks LAUSD into a single vendor relationship. If Ameresco is acquired or underperforms, the district's options are limited.
- No public record of LAUSD comparing Ameresco's proposal against other solar developers or LADWP's own school solar programs.
contracts.cheaper_alternative: LADWP runs solar programs specifically for LAUSD and other public agencies. The district should document whether it compared Ameresco's terms against LADWP's Solar Incentive Program before contracting.
contracts.overlap_note: This project is bond-funded, not general fund. The claimed savings benefit the operating budget but the capital cost comes from voter-approved bonds.

contracts.title: Amira Learning AI Reading Assistant
contracts.description: AI-powered reading instruction and assessment tool. Commitment and spending data from UTLA public records request cited in PESP report.
contracts.plain_english: LAUSD pays Amira Learning for an AI reading tutor. Amira was acquired by Houghton Mifflin Harcourt (HMH), which is owned by private equity firm Veritas Capital — the same firm building a DHS biometric surveillance system via its defense contractor Peraton.
contracts.notes: Per PESP report (UTLA data): $2,129,988 committed, $2,109,888 spent as of July 2025. Contract had 190 days remaining as of July 2025 (~January 2026, now expired). Per PESP: contract was signed in June 2024 in the middle of the AllHere scandal. In a survey of nearly 10,000 educators in LA County, only 12% believed the district would use AI 'in a way that is safe and positive for student learning.' Amira received investments from GSV and Owl Ventures before Veritas Capital acquired it through HMH in 2024.
contracts.keywords: edtech, AI, reading, literacy, artificial intelligence
contracts.questions_to_ask: Why was an AI reading tool contract signed in June 2024 while the AllHere AI scandal was unfolding? Was this a no-bid contract?
contracts.red_flags: Contract signed June 2024 during AllHere AI scandal per PESP report. Parent company Veritas Capital owns Peraton, which is building a DHS biometric surveillance system (HART) that can track ~300 million people in real time. Only 12% of surveyed LA County educators trusted the district to use AI safely (per PESP report).
contracts.sources: PESP (Private Equity Stakeholder Project) report: 'Venture Capital and Private Equity Dominate LAUSD Contracts', April 2026. Data from UTLA public records request.

contracts.title: Defined Learning Project-Based Learning Platform
contracts.description: Project-based learning digital platform. Commitment and spending data from UTLA public records request cited in PESP report.
contracts.plain_english: LAUSD pays Defined Learning for a project-based learning digital platform.
contracts.notes: Per PESP report (UTLA data): $1,352,869 committed, $786,173 spent as of July 2025. Contract had 545 days remaining as of July 2025 (~January 2027). VC-backed per PESP.
contracts.ai_analysis: Defined Learning is a project-based learning platform providing online curriculum units connecting classroom content to career applications. Project-based learning is a well-established pedagogical approach that predates ed-tech platforms — experienced teachers implement PBL without any software. Free and lower-cost alternatives exist including PBLWorks (a nonprofit with free resources), Google Classroom-based PBL frameworks, and state-provided curriculum resources. There is no public evidence that Defined Learning was evaluated against ESSA standards for evidence-based interventions. UNESCO's 2023 review found less than 1 in 5 of the top 100 ed-tech tools used in US classrooms met ESSA evidence requirements.
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- What evidence of student outcome improvement did Defined Learning provide before this contract was signed?
- Did this contract go through LAUSD's UDIPP review process?
- Has LAUSD evaluated free alternatives including PBLWorks resources and state-provided curriculum materials?
- What percentage of LAUSD teachers are actively using this platform?
contracts.red_flags:
- Project-based learning is a teaching methodology that does not require a $1.4M software platform.
- No public evidence this tool was evaluated against ESSA evidence-based intervention standards before purchase.
- Free nonprofit alternatives including PBLWorks provide equivalent curriculum frameworks at no cost.
- Unclear whether this contract went through LAUSD's UDIPP review process for ed-tech procurement.
contracts.sources: PESP (Private Equity Stakeholder Project) report: 'Venture Capital and Private Equity Dominate LAUSD Contracts', April 2026. Data from UTLA public records request.
contracts.cheaper_alternative: PBLWorks — the gold-standard nonprofit for project-based learning — offers free curriculum frameworks, lesson plans, and teacher professional development. A district this size could purchase PBLWorks professional development at a fraction of the cost of a software platform.
contracts.overlap_note: Likely overlaps with the STEM enrichment bench (33 vendors, $10M) and other curriculum contracts. Total LAUSD spending on curriculum supplemental materials is not publicly consolidated.

contracts.title: Outside Legal Counsel Services
contracts.description: Seventy-eight law firms providing outside legal counsel services across administrative, education, business, facilities, general liability, labor, workers compensation, and specialty practice areas. Authorized as an aggregate not-to-exceed amount — individual firm spending is not publicly disclosed.
contracts.funding_source: Liability Self Insurance (55%), General Funds (25%), Bond Funds (20%)
contracts.notes: Corrected: Previously attributed to IT Infrastructure Services contract on same board agenda. Reader review confirmed this $250M belongs to outside legal counsel bench (Item G).
contracts.keywords: legal, outside counsel, law firms, liability, settlements
contracts.ai_analysis: This is a bench contract covering 78 outside law firms approved January 2026 through December 2030. The $250M figure is an aggregate not-to-exceed ceiling — individual firm spending is not disclosed in the public record. The number of firms and aggregate amount warrant scrutiny: who selected these firms, what criteria were used, and how much does each firm actually receive?
contracts.questions_to_ask:
- How much has each of the 78 firms received under this contract?
- What criteria were used to select these firms?
- Are any of these firms connected to board members or district administrators through Form 700 disclosures?
contracts.red_flags:
- Aggregate contract for 78 law firms with no public breakdown of individual firm spending.
- Funded 55% through Liability Self Insurance — the same fund paying sexual abuse settlements.
- No public disclosure of selection criteria for the 78 firms.

campaigns.title: SCHOOLS BEYOND SCREENS CALL TO ACTION!
campaigns.header_text: WE NEED YOUR HELP FOR A UNANIMOUS YES VOTE ON 4/21!
campaigns.body: All eyes are on LAUSD next month to pass the "Using Technology with Intention" resolution which will pave the way for a comprehensive and intentional technology policy in the country's second largest school district, to go into effect this August in time for the 2026-2027 school year. This resolution is the result of over a year of coordinated and consistent effort from parents, teachers and concerned community members who have demanded a safe and science-backed approach to classroom technology. Now is the moment you can help make history!

campaigns.title: Help Avoid an LAUSD Shutdown!
campaigns.header_text: Call to Action: Write to Your Board Member to Support Teachers over Tech
campaigns.body: LAUSD is telling the public it cannot afford to pay its teachers — while simultaneously spending billions on private technology contracts, some of which have ended in bankruptcy, data breaches, and federal criminal investigations. Teachers are on the verge of a strike. Our kids could be out of school. Before that happens, the district needs to hear from parents that we are paying attention — and that we do not accept the premise that there is no money. There is money. The question is how it is being spent. A five-minute email to your board member right now tells them that LAUSD parents are watching, that we stand with our teachers, and that we expect the district to audit its private contracts before cutting services or denying educators a fair wage. Find your board member's email below and make your voice heard.

campaign_templates.email_subject: VOTE YES on “Using Technology with Intention (Res-048-25/26)

campaign_templates.id: \nI am writing to ask that you vote in support of the proposed screen time resolution “Using Technology with Intention” on April 21st and support emergency measures to curb the use of addictive and harmful tech products in LAUSD classrooms. As a parent in your district, I am alarmed by the overuse of screens and edtech in the classroom. I worry–on a daily basis–about the harms of this technology to my child’s cognition, attention, and mental health, and I am begging you to take action.

\nThe research is clear: the rapid and unregulated expansion of technology in our schools has deteriorated learning outcomes over the last decade, and the funds dedicated to these failed initiatives represent money we desperately need for resources like paper, physical books, and teachers. It used to be that we talked about equity in terms of access to technology, but it is critical that we now rethink equity in terms of access to quality instruction. This means putting guardrails around classroom technology so that LA’s public school students do not receive a substandard education, rife with distraction and addictive algorithms.

\nAs has been widely reported in major media outlets, YouTube was recently found liable for the addictive design of its platform and causing harm to children. LAUSD has also now joined hundreds of other school districts in a massive, long-running lawsuit against YouTube and numerous other video-streaming, gaming, and social media platforms, alleging that those platforms have caused harm to the wellbeing of LAUSD students. Yet LAUSD students can still access these products in their classrooms and on their school-issued devices. It is contradictory (and opens the District up to liability) for LAUSD to allow student access to products that the District itself now (accurately) asserts in its lawsuit are harmful and addictive.

\nI therefore ask LAUSD to implement an immediate, emergency ban of YouTube and other video-streaming, gaming, and social media platforms (including, but not limited to Vimeo, Twitch, Roblox, Fortnite, Minecraft, Instagram, TikTok, Discord, Snapchat, Facebook, X, etc.) from student access in the classroom and on school-issued devices.

\nIt is time for LAUSD to prove that it can prioritize children over unethical corporate incentives, and the screen time resolution put forth by Board members Melvoin, Griego, and Ortiz Franklin will provide a necessary first step. Can I count on you to vote YES on 4/21? I look forward to cheering our district on as it sets a national gold standard for student health and academic excellence.

This resolution is the result of over a year of coordinated and consistent effort from parents, teachers and concerned community members who have demanded a safe and science-backed approach to classroom technology that prioritizes teachers over not EdTech companies. campaign_templates.email_subject: VOTE YES on “Using Technology with Intention (Res-048-25/26)

campaign_templates.id: \nI am writing to ask that you vote in support of the proposed screen time resolution “Using Technology with Intention” on April 21st and support emergency measures to curb the use of addictive and harmful tech products in LAUSD classrooms. As a parent in your district, I am alarmed by the overuse of screens and edtech in the classroom. I worry–on a daily basis–about the harms of this technology to my child’s cognition, attention, and mental health, and I am begging you to take action.

\nThe research is clear: the rapid and unregulated expansion of technology in our schools has deteriorated learning outcomes over the last decade, and the funds dedicated to these failed initiatives represent money we desperately need for resources like paper, physical books, and teachers. It used to be that we talked about equity in terms of access to technology, but it is critical that we now rethink equity in terms of access to quality instruction. This means putting guardrails around classroom technology so that LA’s public school students do not receive a substandard education, rife with distraction and addictive algorithms.

\nAs has been widely reported in major media outlets, YouTube was recently found liable for the addictive design of its platform and causing harm to children. LAUSD has also now joined hundreds of other school districts in a massive, long-running lawsuit against YouTube and numerous other video-streaming, gaming, and social media platforms, alleging that those platforms have caused harm to the wellbeing of LAUSD students. Yet LAUSD students can still access these products in their classrooms and on their school-issued devices. It is contradictory (and opens the District up to liability) for LAUSD to allow student access to products that the District itself now (accurately) asserts in its lawsuit are harmful and addictive.

\nI therefore ask LAUSD to implement an immediate, emergency ban of YouTube and other video-streaming, gaming, and social media platforms (including, but not limited to Vimeo, Twitch, Roblox, Fortnite, Minecraft, Instagram, TikTok, Discord, Snapchat, Facebook, X, etc.) from student access in the classroom and on school-issued devices.

\nIt is time for LAUSD to prove that it can prioritize children over unethical corporate incentives, and the screen time resolution put forth by Board members Melvoin, Griego, and Ortiz Franklin will provide a necessary first step. Can I count on you to vote YES on 4/21? I look forward to cheering our district on as it sets a national gold standard for student health and academic excellence.

campaign_templates.campaign_id: SCHOOLS BEYOND SCREENS CALL TO ACTION!

campaign_templates.id: \nAll eyes are on LAUSD next month to pass the “Using Technology with Intention” resolution which will pave the way for a comprehensive and intentional technology policy in the country’s second largest school district, to go into effect this August in time for the 2026-2027 school year. This resolution is the result of over a year of coordinated and consistent effort from parents, teachers and concerned community members who have demanded a safe and science-backed approach to classroom technology that prioritizes teachers over not EdTech companies. Now is the moment you can help make history! Please take a second to click the link below to urge our board member Scott Schmerelson to support this critical resolution!
\nThank you for co-sponsoring the "Using Technology with Intention" resolution. This is a meaningful first step toward addressing the explosion of classroom technology since the pandemic.
\nTo ensure this moves beyond a symbolic gesture, I urge you to strengthen the language in the following ways:
\nAnalog-first instruction: Mandate analog-first approaches for core cognitive tasks. Handwriting and physical books consistently produce higher retention than digital equivalents.
\nA concrete implementation plan: Require the district to publish a Transition Plan before 2026-27, and require schools to publish Technology Use Plans so families can track compliance.

\nA genuine equity framework: Equity in 2026 means every child has access to quality, human-centered instruction, not more passive screens. The "technology equals equity" argument came from the companies selling the products, not from our communities. UNESCO's 2023 Global Education Monitoring Report found that ed-tech actually "supercharges inequity." Without adequate staffing and infrastructure, the same tools that may benefit well-resourced schools actively widen the gap in under-resourced ones. Closer to home, ed-tech surveillance disproportionately harms students of color, undocumented families, and LGBTQ+ youth. Data collected by district-contracted tools has been shared with immigration enforcement. For our families, this is not abstract, it is a genuine safety issue.

\nClearer prohibitions: Explicitly prohibit student-led use of YouTube and generative AI, ban screens entirely for our youngest learners, and establish screen-free social time at lunch and recess for older students.

\nAs has been widely reported in major media outlets, YouTube was recently found liable for the addictive design of its platform and causing harm to children. LAUSD has also now joined hundreds of other school districts in a massive, long-running lawsuit against YouTube and numerous other video-streaming, gaming, and social media platforms, alleging that those platforms have caused harm to the wellbeing of LAUSD students. Yet LAUSD students can still access these products in their classrooms and on their school-issued devices. It is contradictory (and opens the District up to liability) for LAUSD to allow student access to products that the District itself now (accurately) asserts in its lawsuit are harmful and addictive.

\nI therefore ask LAUSD to implement an immediate, emergency ban of YouTube and other video-streaming, gaming, and social media platforms (including, but not limited to Vimeo, Twitch, Roblox, Fortnite, Minecraft, Instagram, TikTok, Discord, Snapchat, Facebook, X, etc.) from student access in the classroom and on school-issued devices. I also call on you to modify the proposed "Using Technology with Intention" resolution to explicitly and affirmatively ban YouTube and the other aforementioned platforms from LAUSD classrooms and school-issued devices.
\nWe have an opportunity to lead the nation in returning to research-backed education. It is time to put children before corporate incentives.
\nThank you for your leadership on this critical issue.

campaign_templates.id: \nAll eyes are on LAUSD next month to pass the “Using Technology with Intention” resolution which will pave the way for a comprehensive and intentional technology policy in the country’s second largest school district, to go into effect this August in time for the 2026-2027 school year. This resolution is the result of over a year of coordinated and consistent effort from parents, teachers and concerned community members who have demanded a safe and science-backed approach to classroom technology that prioritizes teachers over not EdTech companies. Now is the moment you can help make history! Please take a second to click the link below to urge our board member Tanya Ortiz Franklin to support this critical resolution!
\nI am writing as a parent to urge you to approve a fair contract for LAUSD teachers, one that delivers the smaller class sizes and student services our children deserve.

\nLAUSD cites financial solvency to justify holding back from teachers, but millions in taxpayer dollars have already been lost to fraud and predatory contracts. Meanwhile, every family that leaves the public school system takes ADA funding with them, making teacher retention and enrollment directly linked to the district's financial health. If this board is serious about fiscal responsibility, the answer is not to shortchange teachers, it is to audit every dollar going to private companies.
\nParents are not asking for the impossible. We are asking for two things:

\nApprove a fair teacher contract. As housing and cost of living in Los Angeles continue to rise, our teachers cannot afford to live in the communities where they teach. Underpaid teachers in overcrowded classrooms are bad for our children and our schools, and ultimately drive families out of the public school system.

\nConduct an emergency audit of all outside contracts with technology vendors. Since 2022, the district has committed billions of dollars to private companies, including $1.6 billion to ed-tech companies alone. Superintendent Carvalho, who oversaw that spending, is now on paid leave following an FBI raid tied to a $6.2 million AI chatbot contract. Last week, a former LAUSD employee was charged with felony counts in a $22 million pay-to-play tech contract scheme. This is not a series of isolated incidents. It is a pattern of a procurement culture that has consistently prioritized vendors over students and teachers. It also raises an urgent question: what else is LAUSD paying for that we don't yet know about?

\nOur tax dollars belong in classrooms, not in the pockets of CEOs and shareholders who have been milking this district while our educators go underpaid. Our children deserve better. Please do what is right for them and stand with our teachers.

campaign_templates.campaign_id: Write to Your Board Member Today to Avoid a School Shutdown!

campaign_templates.id: \nLAUSD is telling the public it cannot afford to pay its teachers — while simultaneously spending billions on private technology contracts, some of which have ended in bankruptcy, data breaches, and federal criminal investigations. Teachers are on the verge of a strike. Our kids could be out of school. Before that happens, the district needs to hear from parents that we are paying attention — and that we do not accept the premise that there is no money. There is money. The question is how it is being spent. A five-minute email to your board member right now tells them that LAUSD parents are watching, that we stand with our teachers, and that we expect the district to audit its private contracts before cutting services or denying educators a fair wage.
