vendor_profiles.political_donations: Walton Family - major funders of privatization
vendor_profiles.controversies: Walton Family ownership raises ideological concerns; News Corp origins; contract amount never disclosed despite district-wide deployment
vendor_profiles.cost_analysis: Contract amount undisclosed; estimated 0M-0M+ based on scale; requires significant teacher training creating vendor lock-in
vendor_profiles.public_alternative: Public curriculum alternatives exist but integration costs are barrier
vendor_profiles.notes: District-wide deployment across LAUSD via Schoology LMS with SSO integration. Products: mCLASS, Amplify CKLA, Amplify Science, Amplify Reading. Data collection on K-3 students via mCLASS.
vendor_profiles.sources: amplify.com/lausd; LAUSD ITS Digital Learning Tools; Amplify ELA/LAUSD Program Booklet (2021)

vendor_profiles.controversies: Per PESP report (April 2026): Battery Ventures owns LAUSD contractors ContinuumCloud and Welligent. Battery Ventures had a major profit windfall in 2024 by selling its interests in Paragon Solutions — maker of Graphite spyware software — to private equity firm AE Industrial Partners. Prior to the sale, Paragon secured a contract with ICE in October 2024. Members of Congress raised concerns to DHS Secretary Kristi Noem over constitutional rights and civil liberties related to Graphite, which 'can gain unauthorized access into mobile phones without the owner's knowledge or consent, allowing access to encrypted applications, the phone's location data, as well as messages and photographs.' LAUSD had double the expected enrollment decline at the start of 2025-26 due to ICE operations in Los Angeles.
vendor_profiles.notes: Battery Ventures is the parent company of LAUSD contractors ContinuumCloud and its subsidiary Welligent. The firm's connection to Paragon/Graphite spyware and ICE contracts is documented in the PESP April 2026 report.
vendor_profiles.sources: PESP (Private Equity Stakeholder Project) report: 'Venture Capital and Private Equity Dominate LAUSD Contracts', April 2026. Data from UTLA public records request.

vendor_profiles.controversies: Per PESP report (April 2026): Veritas Capital describes itself as 'a premier technology investment firm with an in-depth knowledge and understanding that uniquely focuses on the intersection of technology and government.' Its core business is defense contractors — including Peraton, which is building HART (Homeland Advanced Recognition Technology), a DHS biometric surveillance system that can track nearly 300 million people in real time using DMV data, utility records, social media, license plates, DNA, and facial/eye scans. Veritas just closed a $15 billion fund for defense technology; the California Public Employees' Retirement System committed $400 million. Veritas acquired Cambium Learning Group for $900 million in 2018 and also owns Houghton Mifflin Harcourt (HMH). Together, Cambium and HMH claim to serve 95% and 90% of U.S. school districts respectively. Veritas-owned Anthology (Blackboard) filed for chapter 11 bankruptcy in September 2025 with more than $1 billion in liabilities, losing $80 million in revenue in the two years prior to filing.
vendor_profiles.cost_analysis: Veritas owns two LAUSD contractors via portfolio companies: Explorelearning (via Cambium Learning Group) — $1,004,617 committed per UTLA data; Amira Learning (via HMH) — $2,129,988 committed per UTLA data.
vendor_profiles.notes: Veritas Capital is the ultimate parent of Cambium Learning Group (which owns Lexia Learning and Explorelearning) and Houghton Mifflin Harcourt (which owns Amira Learning and Istation). The firm's primary business is defense and government technology, not education.
vendor_profiles.sources: PESP (Private Equity Stakeholder Project) report: 'Venture Capital and Private Equity Dominate LAUSD Contracts', April 2026. Data from UTLA public records request.

vendor_profiles.key_executives: CEO: Tim Murphy (also an operating partner at Shore Capital Partners)
vendor_profiles.controversies: Per PESP report (April 2026): Stepping Stones Group is one of the largest companies providing behavioral health services to children. It operates under three vendor names in LAUSD contracts: Stepping Stones (special education), CBEST/Center for Behavioral, Educational, and Social Therapies (speech and language, acquired 2022), and Progressus Therapy (via Invo Healthcare, acquired 2025). Together, the Stepping Stones and CBEST contracts are worth more than $26 million per the PESP report. In 2024, Stepping Stones agreed to pay $4.25 million to settle a class action lawsuit alleging widespread wage and hour violations — including failure to pay minimum wages, failure to provide meal and rest periods, and untimely payment of wages. The company denied all claims while agreeing to settle. The company has acquired at least 21 smaller companies since Leonard Green and Partners acquired it in December 2021. Prior owner Shore Capital Partners (2014–2018) made a 7x return on investment. Shore Capital founder Justin Ishbia is a billionaire with majority stakes in the Chicago White Sox, Phoenix Suns, and Phoenix Mercury. Parent firm Leonard Green and Partners had $75 billion in assets under management as of December 2024. Leonard Green also previously owned Prospect Medical Holdings, which filed for bankruptcy in January 2025 with over $1 billion in liabilities after Leonard Green and minority owners extracted approximately $658 million in fees and dividends while the company took a $603 million loss. A July 2025 Senate Budget Committee report found Leonard Green board meetings focused on 'profit maximization tactics — cost cutting, increasing patient volume, and managing labor expenses — with little to no discussion of patient outcomes or quality of care.' Leonard Green also owned Joann when it filed for bankruptcy, laying off 19,000 people and closing 500 stores.
vendor_profiles.cost_analysis: Per PESP report: Stepping Stones and CBEST contracts together worth more than $26 million. Progressus Therapy is an additional LAUSD contractor in the speech language category.
vendor_profiles.public_alternative: In-house district special education staff (nurses, paraeducators, speech-language professionals)
vendor_profiles.notes: Stepping Stones operates under multiple vendor names in LAUSD: 'Stepping Stones Group' (special education), 'CBEST' (speech and language), and 'Progressus Therapy' (speech language, via Invo Healthcare acquisition 2025). All are ultimately owned by private equity firm Leonard Green and Partners.
vendor_profiles.sources: PESP (Private Equity Stakeholder Project) report: 'Venture Capital and Private Equity Dominate LAUSD Contracts', April 2026. Data from UTLA public records request.

vendor_profiles.controversies: Per PESP report (April 2026): LAUSD began contracting with Age of Learning in 2023 after Carvalho became superintendent, committing $3.5 million for two years. Carvalho had previously brought Age of Learning to Miami-Dade County Public Schools starting in 2016, when Debra Kerr was Head of Sales for the company. Kerr later claimed to have facilitated the AllHere AI chatbot contract with LAUSD — the contract at the center of the federal investigation into Carvalho. This follows a pattern in which Carvalho brought Miami-Dade vendors to LAUSD.
vendor_profiles.cost_analysis: Per UTLA records request (cited in PESP report): $3,501,946 total commitment, $3,497,000 spent as of July 2025.
vendor_profiles.notes: Connected to AllHere/Carvalho network via former employee Debra Kerr. See AllHere contract for related investigation details.
vendor_profiles.sources: PESP (Private Equity Stakeholder Project) report: 'Venture Capital and Private Equity Dominate LAUSD Contracts', April 2026. Data from UTLA public records request.

news_items.title: Felony Charges Filed Against Former LAUSD Employee and Vendor Owner in Alleged $22 Million Contracting Scheme
news_items.summary: A former LAUSD IT employee and a technology vendor owner face felony charges for allegedly directing over $22 million in district contracts through an illegal pay-to-play arrangement, siphoning taxpayer funds from schools.

news_items.title: AllHere Set Meeting With LAUSD Leaders Months Before Landing $6.2M Chatbot Deal
news_items.summary: The AI chatbot deal, the consultant who called the meeting and her close friend, L.A. schools chief Albert Carvalho, are now all linked to FBI probe.

news_items.title: LAUSD Board’s Oversight Failure
news_items.summary: Carvalho violated Prop 28, a voter-approved measure requiring new arts education funding to supplement (not replace) existing spending. A Board Informative he authored in August 2024 explicitly states the district used Prop 28 funds to cover existing staff — a direct contradiction of what voters were promised, and of Carvalho's own public statements.

news_items.title: Venture Capital and Private Equity Dominate LAUSD Contracts
news_items.summary: A new report finds that 86% of LAUSD digital instruction tool contracts ($255M) went to VC/PE-backed companies since 2022. 72% of active tech contracts ($1.6B) were authorized under emergency no-bid powers granted to Superintendent Carvalho. LAUSD has approved $10 billion in contracts with outside vendors since Carvalho took over in 2022.

report_findings.title: 86% of digital instruction contracts went to VC/PE-backed companies
report_findings.detail: From January 2022 to June 2025, at least 86% of all LAUSD contract commitments with companies that sell digital instruction tools ($255 million of $297 million total) went to venture capital or private equity-backed companies.

report_findings.title: 72% of active tech contracts authorized under emergency no-bid powers
report_findings.detail: 72% of all currently active technology contracts ($1.6 billion) were authorized after Superintendent Carvalho requested and was granted emergency authority to obtain no-bid contracts following a September 2022 ransomware attack.

report_findings.title: $10 billion in outside vendor contracts since Carvalho arrived
report_findings.detail: Since Carvalho started in 2022, LAUSD has approved $10 billion in contracts with outside vendors. The district is on track to spend $1.5 billion on services and other operating expenses (contracting) this year, which is $401 million over budget.

report_findings.title: Nearly 70% of VC/PE digital tool vendors exceeded contract maximums
report_findings.detail: Of VC and private equity-owned digital instruction companies, nearly 70% have been paid more than the original contract commitment amount, with $265 million in total spending exceeding $255 million in commitments.

report_findings.title: $723 million in special education subcontracting since 2022
report_findings.detail: Since 2022, LAUSD has committed $723 million across 119 contracts with 93 vendors for work that may be UTLA bargaining unit work, including nurses, paraeducators, speech and language professionals, and tutors.
